query: formation of multiple roots in multirooted teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Dilaceration describes an unusually abrupt angle between tooth crown and root or within the root itself, resulting from trauma during the development of the tooth. Further anomalies of the dental hard tissues include gemination, where a single tooth forms two crowns. This is a consequence of the tooth bud's partial division, resulting in a bifid crown but retaining the normal number of expected teeth within the dental arch. [30] However, fusion leads to one less tooth within the dental arch as it comprises two separate developing tooth germs which join together. [31] Concrescence is a rare phenomenon where adjacent teeth are united by cementum. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the mandible, central and lateral incisors have single roots, with central incisors often lacking apical curvature. [6] If present in either tooth, apical curvature tends to be distally inclined. [6] Mandibular canines usually present with single roots, but one unique difference between maxillary and mandibular canines is that mandibular canines may have bifurcated roots similar to those seen in maxillary first premolars. [6] The mandibular first and second premolars typically present with single roots but may have bifurcated roots in rare situations. The root tips are often inclined distally, and the mandibular second premolar's root is usually longer than the first premolar's. [7] The mandibular first and second molars usually have two distally tilted roots: a mesial and a distal root. The mandibular second molar's roots are usually shorter with less splay than the first molar roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gemination: It is the condition that arises when two teeth develop from the same follicle and attempt to divide, resulting in a single tooth with two incompletely separated crowns or one incompletely separated crown but with a single root and root canal. It is sometimes also referred to as double teeth or dental twinning and is more commonly associated with the maxillary anterior region. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the dental anomalies of reduced or increased number of teeth. Identify the etiologies of dental anomalies of the number of teeth. Differentiate the differences between the dental anomalies of the number of teeth. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination outcomes in patients with anomalies in the number of teeth. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the morphogenesis and anatomical considerations of tooth roots. Review indications, contraindications, and preparatory considerations for the extraction of tooth roots. Summarize techniques commonly employed in the extraction of tooth roots. Outline potential complications associated with root extraction surgery and explain the importance of clinical proficiency in managing fractured and retained root fragments. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fusion occurs when 2 developing tooth buds are close enough to merge, forming a single unit. This process may result from the proximity of adjacent tooth buds or the presence of supernumerary teeth. Studies suggest that signaling mediated by the Jagged2 gene may influence tooth development and contribute to fusion. [19] Fusion should be distinguished from gemination, a process in which a single tooth bud attempts to divide, producing a tooth with a partially split crown that appears larger or bifid. In fusion, the total tooth count decreases because 2 teeth merge into 1, whereas in gemination, the overall number of teeth remains unchanged.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Root morphology tends to follow a pattern in most individuals. In the maxilla, central and lateral incisors traditionally have single, straight, and typically conical roots. [2] Maxillary canines are also single-rooted and are the longest of all tooth roots. The maxillary first premolar often has a long root trunk with bifurcated buccal and palatal roots, but single root variants exist. [3] Maxillary premolar roots are typically thin. [3] Because of the length of the root trunk, which often spans half the length of the root, maxillary first premolars are commonly associated with inadvertent root tip fractures during extraction. Unlike the maxillary first premolar, the maxillary second premolar is usually single-rooted and rarely presents with two roots or a bifurcated root phenotypically.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: With the invention of the rotary surgical drill, the transition from simple to surgical exodontia has never been easier. Some say surgical drills are one of the greatest innovations in modern exodontia. [14] They minimize operating time and allow the surgeon to access points of the tooth that were previously inaccessible through simple exodontia methods. [14] When indicated, cortical bone can be drilled away, or roots can be sectioned to increase purchase points and allow for a more profound apical orientation of elevators. When multi-rooted teeth require sectioning with a drill, it is advantageous to treat each root as a single-rooted tooth when extracting, as it promotes less trauma to the supporting alveolus. [21] Maxillary molar roots are traditionally sectioned at the furcation level in a Y or T configuration. [20] In contrast, mandibular molar roots and other 2-rooted teeth are sectioned horizontally through their respective furcations. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple supernumerary teeth rarely appear in patients without an associated syndrome or developmental disorder. In contrast, a single supernumerary tooth represents a relatively common dental anomaly and typically does not require further diagnostic workup. Supernumerary teeth most frequently occur in the anterior maxilla, mandibular premolar region, or around the third molars. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fusion: It is the condition where two teeth develop from two different dental follicles, but during the process of formation, they fuse partially or completely, resulting in a single large tooth or tooth fused completely at crown or root only. The degree of fusion depends on the stage of tooth formation at which fusion occurs. When fusion begins before the calcification stage, complete union occurs, and when it occurs at a late stage, incomplete union occurs. It is more common in the anterior region of the deciduous dentition. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Supernumerary teeth, if malpositioned, can prevent the normal eruption of developing teeth, which would require removal, a potentially invasive procedure. The multiple supernumerary teeth are generally indicative of an underlying syndromic or developmental condition, which the appropriate clinician should evaluate.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: An additional cusp called the cusp of Carabelli is sometimes located on the mesio-palatal surface of permanent maxillary first molars and is rarely present on the other permanent molars. Although less common, an accessory cusp can protrude from the cingulum of the primary or permanent anterior teeth, referred to as the talon cusp. [26] Macrodontia and microdontia are rare shape anomalies of dentition, where teeth are larger or smaller than average. [27] [28] Taurodontism is an abnormality of tooth morphology that often occurs in multi-rooted teeth and is characterized by a short root and an enlarged body containing an equally enlarged pulp chamber. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillary first molars typically have two buccal roots and one larger palatal root. [4] Roots may splay and have variable curvature. [4] Both the maxillary first and second molars have distal root inclinations, with the second molar often to a greater degree than the first. The second molar also traditionally has three roots that are usually less splayed than the first molar. [5] In some cases, the roots may fuse on the buccal aspect. The maxillary third molar has the most significant anatomical variability of all teeth. The roots of the maxillary third molar tend to be fused and shorter than the maxillary first and second molars. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The now infected and necrotic root canal system is a habitat that allows for the growth and establishment of a mixed, predominately anaerobic flora, which mainly grows in sessile biofilms and aggregates or co-aggregates. [6] The microbial invaders or their by-products can advance into the periapex of the root. In response, the host mounts a defensive mechanism through various cells, intracellular mediators, and humoral antibodies. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Dens invaginatus has various clinical presentations, which depend on the severity of the in-folding or the phase of morphodifferentiation where the invagination occurs. Several classifications exist to describe the extent of the in-folded tissue. The most utilized system is the Oehlers classification due to its ease of application. [18] In Type 1, the invagination ends as a blind sac and is limited to the coronal aspect of the tooth. Type 2 invagination extends beyond the CEJ and is retained within the main root canal of the tooth. Type 3 occurs when the invagination of dental tissue extends throughout the interior aspect of the root canal, sometimes even reaching the apical third of the tooth, potentially resulting in multiple apical foramina. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact is identified as the event that can cause loss of teeth, with the tooth loss directly linked to the accident itself. Injury (إصابة) is organized hierarchically, encompassing superficial injury; contusion (إصابة سطحية؛ كدمة) as a parent of the generic late‑effect category of unspecified injury (الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة غير محددة). This unspecified‑injury category includes numerous region‑specific late‑effect entities—upper extremity, neck and trunk, head, lower extremity—and broader chronic sequelae such as late effects of fracture of multiple bones, late effects of fracture of spine and/or trunk without spinal cord lesion, late effects of burns, etching and freezing, and late effects of injury involving multiple body regions. The late‑effect hierarchy also contains the SMQ “المجموعة العدائية/العدوان” (SMQ), which itself comprises superficial injury; contusion and the behavior‑related concept سادية ممزوجة بتعذيب الذات (sadistic‑masochistic self‑harm), linking

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.
